zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir George Ker of Samuelston於1450年出生在蘇格蘭哈丁頓史爾哈丁頓時，他的父親Sir Andrew Kerr of Auldbounburn and Cessford30歲，母親Margaret Douglas25歲。他和Mariëtte Sinclair至少有1個女兒。他去世於1500年11月6日在蘇格蘭哈丁頓史爾薩苗爾斯頓享年50歲。

nameMeanings.name: Ker
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Carr  .

Hungarian (Kér): from the name of the ancient Hungarian tribe Kér. The tribe settled, after the Magyar conquest of the Carpathian basin in the 9th–10th centuries, in what is now known as Békés County, but King Steven I resettled the tribe in royal estates, far away from their original residence. Thus the 42 villages named after the Kér tribe are scattered around in Hungary.
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 寇, see   Kou   1.

variants.name: Carr
meanings: Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Carra ‘descendant of Carra’, a personal name from the adjective corr ‘pointed’, explained as meaning ‘spear’. As an Ulster surname, Carr was often confused with Scottish Kerr .

Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Mac Giolla Chathair, a Donegal name meaning ‘son of Giolla Cathair’ or ‘the servant (i.e. devotee) of Saint Cathar’. Cathar was a priest and bishop, otherwise unknown.
Irish: in Galway, a shortened Anglicized form of Mac Giolla Chéire, see Keary .

nameMeanings.name: George
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir George Ker of Samuelston was born in 1450, in Haddington, Haddingtonshire, Scotland, his father, Sir Andrew Kerr of Auldbounburn and Cessford, was 30 and his mother, Margaret Douglas, was 25. He had at least 1 daughter with Mariëtte Sinclair. He died on 6 November 1500, in Samuelston, Haddingtonshire, Scotland, at the age of 50.
